content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Over the last few months, my team’s engineering velocity has looked like a vertical line. We stopped treating AI as a glorified autocomplete tool and leaned fully into the agentic era. We gave tools like Claude Code, Cursor, and various terminal daemons direct access to our local files, hooked them into our telemetry systems, and let them autonomously find and patch production bugs. It felt like playing a video game on cheat mode.
Then came the morning of June 23, 2026, and a new security vulnerability stripped away all that optimism.
The security community is calling it «Agentjacking.» It isn’t just another theoretical bug or a minor patch requirement; it is the first major, purpose-built attack class engineered explicitly to turn autonomous coding assistants into internal threat actors. Over 2,300 engineering organizations have already been flagged as compromised, and the worst part is that our industry standard defenses – firewalls, dependency checkers, token encryption – are completely blind to it.
If you are currently running an AI agent with terminal execution rights on your machine, you need to understand how this exploit works before you open your IDE tomorrow morning.
The underlying mechanism of Agentjacking is an elegant, terrifying spin on indirect prompt injection.
As developers, we are trained to sanitize user inputs inside our applications. We escape strings, validate schemas, and ensure that malicious…
